meta.title: Student Health Academy
meta.description: A cinematic, mission-based health literacy academy for high school students: first aid, STIs, vaccines, and critical thinking.

hub.title: Student Health Academy
hub.subtitle: A mission-based health field guide for high school students. Learn what to do before help arrives, how to protect your body and relationships, and how to separate evidence from rumor.
hub.disclaimer: Educational field guide only: this is not medical care, diagnosis, or emergency instruction from a clinician. If someone may be in immediate danger, call emergency services. For sexual health, consent, or privacy concerns, seek a trusted adult, school nurse, doctor, clinic, or local helpline.

emergency101.title: Emergency signals: calling for help
emergency101.description: In an emergency, the first minute is a bridge between chaos and help. Your job is not to become a doctor. Your job is to stay safe, call the right number, and give clear information that helps trained responders find the scene fast.
emergency101.tip1: Lead with location: school name, street, building entrance, landmark, or GPS pin. A perfect medical description is useless if help cannot find you.
emergency101.tip2: Say what happened in one sentence: “A student collapsed and is not responding,” or “There is heavy bleeding from the arm.” Short, concrete details help dispatchers act.
emergency101.tip3: Put the phone on speaker if you can. The dispatcher may coach you while someone else gets an adult, opens a gate, or clears space.
emergency101.tip4: Never film an emergency for attention. Protect privacy, create space, and stay with the person if it is safe until help arrives.
emergency101.fact: Emergency systems are designed around fast, accurate information. Clear location plus a brief description can shorten the time between the call and professional care.
emergency101.action: Create a phone note called “Emergency info” with your home address, school address, and one nearby landmark for each.
emergency101.scenario: During sports practice, a classmate suddenly drops to the ground. The gym gets loud. One person is recording. No adult is beside the student yet.
emergency101.challenge: Write a 15-second emergency script: who you are, where you are, what happened, and what you need.

bleedingBasics.title: Bleeding: pressure buys time
bleedingBasics.description: Blood can make people panic, but the first response is simple: protect yourself, press firmly, and get help. Pressure is not dramatic, but it is one of the most powerful first-aid actions a bystander can take.
bleedingBasics.tip1: Protect yourself if possible: gloves are best, but a plastic bag or folded cloth is better than bare hands if blood is visible.
bleedingBasics.tip2: Press directly on the wound with the cleanest cloth available. Strong, steady pressure matters more than constantly checking how it looks.
bleedingBasics.tip3: If blood soaks through, add more cloth on top. Removing the first layer can pull away early clots and restart bleeding.
bleedingBasics.tip4: Call emergency services for heavy bleeding, spurting blood, deep wounds, or bleeding that does not slow with firm pressure.
bleedingBasics.fact: Many severe external bleeds can be slowed before professionals arrive. The difference is often whether someone starts firm pressure immediately.
bleedingBasics.action: Find the first-aid kit at home or school and identify what could be used for clean pressure in an emergency.
bleedingBasics.scenario: After school, someone cuts their forearm on broken glass. Their sleeve is wet with blood, and friends are afraid to touch anything.
bleedingBasics.challenge: Practice saying the rule out loud: “Press. Add layers. Keep pressing. Call for help.”

burnsBasics.title: Burns: cool the heat, protect the skin
burnsBasics.description: A burn keeps damaging tissue after the heat source is gone. The goal is to cool the skin safely, avoid folk remedies that trap heat, and know when a burn needs urgent medical care.
burnsBasics.tip1: Cool running water is the first move for many minor burns. Keep it gentle and cool, not icy.
burnsBasics.tip2: Skip butter, oil, toothpaste, flour, or egg. They can trap heat, hide the injury, and raise infection risk.
burnsBasics.tip3: Remove tight rings, bracelets, or clothing near the burn if they are not stuck, because swelling can happen quickly.
burnsBasics.tip4: Get urgent care for burns on the face, hands, genitals, large areas, deep burns, chemical burns, electrical burns, or trouble breathing.
burnsBasics.fact: Early cooling can reduce pain and depth of injury. The best burn care often begins before anyone reaches a clinic.
burnsBasics.action: Teach one person the burn rule: cool water first, cover loosely, no kitchen remedies.
burnsBasics.scenario: A younger sibling touches a hot pan. An older relative reaches for toothpaste because “that always helped before.”
burnsBasics.challenge: Make a two-line poster: “Cool the burn. Do not cover it with food or paste.”

stiBasics.title: STIs: facts without shame
stiBasics.description: Sexually transmitted infections are health conditions, not character judgments. Some are curable, some are manageable, and many have no symptoms at first. Accurate information protects people from stigma and from delayed care.
stiBasics.tip1: Symptoms are not a reliable test. Many STIs can be silent while still affecting health or spreading to partners.
stiBasics.tip2: Condoms and dental dams reduce risk when used correctly, but no tool works if someone is pressured or unable to use it safely.
stiBasics.tip3: Testing is responsible. It is something people do to care for themselves and others, not something to gossip about.
stiBasics.tip4: Ask clinics directly about confidentiality: who can see results, what age rules apply, and whether services are youth-friendly.
stiBasics.fact: Untreated infections such as chlamydia and gonorrhea can lead to serious complications, but testing and treatment are effective when people get care early.
stiBasics.action: Identify one trustworthy sexual-health source in your country: a health ministry page, youth clinic, school nurse, or helpline.
stiBasics.scenario: A friend says, “I would know if someone had an STI just by looking.” Another student quietly stops talking.
stiBasics.challenge: Prepare a stigma-free reply: “You cannot always tell. Testing is normal, private, and responsible.”

consentBasics.title: Consent: the clearest yes
consentBasics.description: Consent is not a technicality. It is the foundation of safety, respect, and trust. A healthy relationship gives every person the freedom to say yes, no, wait, or stop without fear of punishment.
consentBasics.tip1: Consent must be freely chosen. Pressure, threats, guilt, alcohol, drugs, or power differences can make “yes” unsafe or invalid.
consentBasics.tip2: Silence is not consent. Freezing, going quiet, or not fighting back is not the same as wanting something.
consentBasics.tip3: Consent is ongoing. Someone can change their mind even if they agreed earlier, even in a relationship.
consentBasics.tip4: Digital boundaries count. Repeatedly asking for photos, sharing private images, or threatening exposure is not respect.
consentBasics.fact: Consent education reduces harm because it gives students practical language for boundaries before pressure becomes danger.
consentBasics.action: Choose a trusted person or service you could contact if you or a friend felt pressured, unsafe, or threatened.
consentBasics.scenario: Someone keeps asking for private photos after being told no, then says, “If you loved me, you would.”
consentBasics.challenge: Write a boundary script you could send quickly: “No. Stop asking. I am leaving this conversation.”

chokingBasics.title: Choking: recognize the silent emergency
chokingBasics.description: Choking can turn a normal lunch table into a life-threatening emergency in seconds. The safest response begins with recognizing whether the person can cough or breathe, calling for help, and using trained techniques when needed.
chokingBasics.tip1: If the person is coughing hard, encourage coughing and stay close. A strong cough is the body’s best tool.
chokingBasics.tip2: If they cannot speak, cough, cry, or breathe, treat it as an emergency and call for help immediately.
chokingBasics.tip3: Back blows and abdominal thrusts should be learned from a certified first-aid instructor. Good technique matters.
chokingBasics.tip4: After a serious choking episode, medical evaluation is wise even if the person seems better.
chokingBasics.fact: Choking is often quiet, not dramatic. Bystanders who recognize the signs quickly can make the difference before professionals arrive.
chokingBasics.action: Ask your school who is trained in first aid and where students can learn CPR or choking response.
chokingBasics.scenario: In the cafeteria, a student suddenly stands up, grabs their throat, and cannot answer when friends ask what is wrong.
chokingBasics.challenge: Find out where your school keeps emergency contacts and who is first-aid trained.

stiTesting.title: Testing and treatment: privacy, proof, care
stiTesting.description: Testing replaces fear with facts. It can feel intimidating, but clinics do this every day. The strongest move is to ask clear questions about privacy, testing options, treatment, and partner safety.
stiTesting.tip1: Tests may use urine, blood, or swabs. A clinician can explain what is needed based on exposure and symptoms.
stiTesting.tip2: Be honest with clinicians. They are not there to judge; accurate information helps them choose the right tests.
stiTesting.tip3: If treatment is prescribed, take it exactly as directed and ask when it is safe to have sex again.
stiTesting.tip4: Partners may need testing or treatment. Clinics can help with safe, private ways to communicate this.
stiTesting.fact: Early testing and treatment can prevent long-term complications and reduce transmission in a community.
stiTesting.action: Write down three clinic questions: “Is this confidential?”, “Which tests do I need?”, “When should I retest?”
stiTesting.scenario: A friend is worried after possible exposure but says, “If I go to a clinic, everyone will know.”
stiTesting.challenge: Build a privacy checklist a student could use before making an appointment.

vaccineLiteracy.title: Vaccines: training the immune system
vaccineLiteracy.description: Vaccines are not magic shields; they are training sessions for the immune system. They help the body recognize danger before a real infection has the chance to cause serious harm.
vaccineLiteracy.tip1: A vaccine schedule is a map. It shows which protections are recommended at different ages and when catch-up doses may be needed.
vaccineLiteracy.tip2: A sore arm or mild fever can happen because the immune system is responding. Clinics can tell you what is normal and what is not.
vaccineLiteracy.tip3: Vaccines are monitored for safety before and after approval. Serious allergic reactions are rare, and clinics are prepared for them.
vaccineLiteracy.tip4: If you missed a dose, do not guess. Ask a clinician or official schedule about catch-up options.
vaccineLiteracy.fact: High vaccine coverage protects individuals and also helps protect people who cannot be vaccinated for medical reasons.
vaccineLiteracy.action: Check your vaccine record with a parent, guardian, doctor, or school nurse and list one question to ask.
vaccineLiteracy.scenario: A group chat claims a vaccine “rewrites your DNA.” The post has dramatic music and no source.
vaccineLiteracy.challenge: Find the same vaccine on a public health website and write one evidence-based reply.

vpdExamples.title: Diseases vaccines can prevent
vpdExamples.description: A vaccine card is not just paperwork. It is a record of protection against diseases that once injured or killed far more young people: measles, hepatitis B, HPV-related cancers, and more.
vpdExamples.tip1: Measles spreads through the air and can cause pneumonia, brain swelling, and death. It is not just a rash.
vpdExamples.tip2: HPV vaccination can prevent several cancers later in life. It works best before exposure to the virus.
vpdExamples.tip3: Hepatitis B can become a chronic liver infection. Vaccination is a major tool for prevention.
vpdExamples.tip4: Disease stories travel fast online. Evidence from health agencies travels slower but is far more reliable.
vpdExamples.fact: Before widespread vaccination, measles killed millions globally. Vaccines changed what childhood risk looked like.
vpdExamples.action: Choose one vaccine-preventable disease and make a three-bullet “why it matters” card.
vpdExamples.scenario: Students are preparing for a school trip, but nobody agrees which vaccines matter for teens.
vpdExamples.challenge: Make a pocket guide: disease, how it spreads, vaccine that helps prevent it.

firstAidReview.title: First aid command: putting it together
firstAidReview.description: At the national stage, first aid becomes decision-making under pressure: protect yourself, get help, choose the right basic action, and keep the scene calm enough for professionals to take over.
firstAidReview.tip1: Scene safety comes first. You cannot help if you become the second injured person.
firstAidReview.tip2: Call early when the situation may be serious. Dispatchers would rather guide a cautious caller than arrive too late.
firstAidReview.tip3: Match action to problem: pressure for bleeding, cool water for burns, trained response for choking, space and reassurance for shock.
firstAidReview.tip4: Leadership can be simple: point to one person and say, “You call emergency services. You get a teacher. You clear space.”
firstAidReview.fact: In emergencies, calm role assignment reduces confusion and speeds up response, especially in crowded places like schools.
firstAidReview.action: Write a 60-second classroom emergency plan: caller, adult, space, supplies, comfort.
firstAidReview.scenario: One student is burned, another is bleeding, and the room is filling with noise. You are the first person thinking clearly.
firstAidReview.challenge: Draft a four-role emergency plan your class could practice safely with a teacher.

stiMyths.title: STI myths: replacing gossip with evidence
stiMyths.description: Myths thrive where shame is loud and facts are quiet. Health literacy means refusing gossip, protecting privacy, and pointing people toward testing and care instead of blame.
stiMyths.tip1: Myth: You can always tell who has an STI. Reality: many infections have no visible signs.
stiMyths.tip2: Myth: Only “certain people” get STIs. Reality: exposure is about biology and behavior, not someone’s worth.
stiMyths.tip3: Myth: Two condoms are safer than one. Reality: friction can make condoms tear; one used correctly is safer.
stiMyths.tip4: Myth: Borrowed antibiotics are enough. Reality: wrong treatment can fail and contribute to resistance.
stiMyths.fact: Stigma delays testing. When communities talk about STIs as health issues, people seek care earlier.
stiMyths.action: Practice one sentence that shuts down gossip while protecting privacy.
stiMyths.scenario: A rumor spreads about a student who might need testing. People are laughing and guessing names.
stiMyths.challenge: Write a response that says: stop gossiping, respect privacy, use real health information.

sourceLiteracy.title: Health information: evidence in a noisy world
sourceLiteracy.description: Your feed is a health battlefield: miracle cures, fear posts, influencer advice, and real science all compete for attention. The skill is not knowing everything. The skill is knowing what deserves trust.
sourceLiteracy.tip1: Ask who made the claim, what evidence they show, and whether recognized health organizations agree.
sourceLiteracy.tip2: Beware of phrases like “doctors hate this,” “secret cure,” or “share before it is deleted.” They are designed to bypass critical thinking.
sourceLiteracy.tip3: Look for dates. Health guidance can change as evidence improves.
sourceLiteracy.tip4: Before sharing, pause. A false health post can spread fear faster than a virus.
sourceLiteracy.fact: Health literacy is linked to better decisions because people can find, understand, and use reliable information under pressure.
sourceLiteracy.action: Compare one viral health claim with an official health source and write the difference in plain language.
sourceLiteracy.scenario: A video promises a miracle cure, uses dramatic music, and says hospitals are hiding the truth.
sourceLiteracy.challenge: Run the three-question source test: Who says it? What proof? What do health agencies say?

capstoneMix.title: Capstone: lead a health mission
capstoneMix.description: The final mission asks you to combine everything: first response, sexual health, vaccines, consent, and evidence. The goal is not to be the expert in every room. The goal is to become the person who knows how to find help, reduce harm, and keep facts alive.
capstoneMix.tip1: Start with safety: emergency risks, pressure, consent, privacy, and reliable adults or services.
capstoneMix.tip2: Use prevention as a stack: vaccines, safer choices, testing, hygiene, trusted information, and timely care.
capstoneMix.tip3: Respect privacy. A helpful student does not expose someone’s health story to prove a point.
capstoneMix.tip4: Teach in plain language. If your younger sibling can understand it, your message is stronger.
capstoneMix.fact: Peer-led health education works best when it is practical, respectful, and connected to real services.
capstoneMix.action: Design a one-page school health mission plan with four stations: first aid, consent, vaccines, and fact-checking.
capstoneMix.scenario: You are asked to help plan a school health week. Students say they do not want another boring lecture.
capstoneMix.challenge: Create a four-station health week agenda with one activity, one trusted resource, and one takeaway per station.

q0.question: What should you tell an emergency dispatcher first if possible?
q0.a1: Where you are and what happened
q0.explanation: Location and a brief description of the emergency help them send the right resources quickly.

q1.question: For heavy bleeding that soaks through a cloth, you should:
q1.a0: Remove the cloth to look at the wound
q1.a1: Add another layer on top and keep pressing
q1.a2: Wait an hour to see if it stops
q1.explanation: Removing the cloth can disturb clots. Add pressure on top until help arrives.

q2.question: For a small fresh burn, the best first step is usually:
q2.a1: Cool with running cool water for several minutes
q2.explanation: Cool running water reduces heat in the tissue. Butter traps heat and increases infection risk.

q3.question: Why might someone get an STI test even without symptoms?
q3.a0: Tests are only for people with symptoms
q3.a1: Many STIs can be silent but still harmful or spreadable
q3.explanation: Many infections have no early symptoms; testing finds them so you can get care and protect partners.

q0.question: If an adult suddenly cannot speak or breathe and holds their throat, you should:
q0.a2: Give them a glass of water
q0.explanation: Complete airway blockage is an emergency — trained help and EMS are needed urgently.

q1.question: If prescribed antibiotics for an STI, you should:
q1.explanation: Stopping early can fail to kill the bacteria and contribute to resistance. Never share antibiotics.

q2.question: Vaccines mainly work by:
q2.a1: Training the immune system to recognize a germ
q2.explanation: Vaccines prepare your immune system so a future encounter with the real germ is much safer.

q3.question: Measles is:
q3.a1: A serious disease that vaccines have greatly reduced
q3.explanation: Measles can cause severe complications; vaccination is a key reason cases dropped worldwide.

q0.question: In a medical emergency at school, the best first step is usually:
q0.a1: Get adult help and emergency services if needed
q0.a2: Wait until the end of class
q0.explanation: Trained adults and EMS are equipped to respond; time matters in emergencies.

q1.question: A reliable place to check vaccine facts is often:
q1.a1: Your country's ministry of health or WHO
q1.explanation: Official health agencies review evidence and update guidance — use them to verify claims.

q2.question: Two condoms at once are:
q2.a1: Not recommended — friction can cause breakage
q2.explanation: Using one condom correctly is safer than double-layering, which increases tear risk.

q3.question: Health literacy means:
q3.a1: Finding, understanding, and using reliable health information
q3.explanation: It is about skills to navigate real information and services, not encyclopedic memorization.

privacy.successDeleted: Your data has been scrubbed. Goodbye for now.
privacy.disclaimer: Redi Health is not a medical device and does not provide medical advice. All AI-generated content is for educational purposes only. Always consult a qualified healthcare professional for medical decisions.

collect.title: What we collect
collect.body: Account, preferences, lesson progress, mood and water check-ins you log, and audit records of high-value actions. We do not collect raw AI prompts or Field Lab notes — those stay on your device.

use.title: How we use it
use.body: We personalise the academy, prevent abuse, and improve content. We never sell your data. We use anonymous, aggregated analytics when enabled (PostHog) and respect Do-Not-Track.

storage.title: Where it lives
storage.body: Your account and progress are stored in EU-region Supabase Postgres. Server logs and error reports use EU-region Sentry. Field Lab notes, streaks, and XP are device-local.

processors.title: Third-party processors
processors.body: OpenAI processes AI prompts in real time (we scrub PII first). Sentry handles error tracking. Resend handles email. PostHog handles optional analytics. Upstash handles rate-limit counters. Each is contractually bound by a data-processing agreement.

rights.title: Your GDPR rights
rights.body: You can access, port, correct, or delete your data at any time. Use the buttons above. We respond to requests within 30 days. You can also lodge a complaint with your local data-protection authority.

transfers.title: International transfers
transfers.body: Some processors operate globally (e.g. OpenAI). Where data leaves the EEA we rely on Standard Contractual Clauses. You can use the academy without enabling AI features.

deletion.title: How deletion works
deletion.body: When you click Delete, we immediately scrub identifying fields (email, name, phone). After 30 days, all rows tied to your account are permanently removed. Pseudonymised statistics may persist for population health research.

contact.title: Data Protection Officer
contact.body: Email dpo@redi.healthcare with any questions or formal requests. We acknowledge within 72 hours and resolve within 30 days, as required by GDPR.

sos.buttonAria: Emergency SOS — open emergency numbers and first aid
sos.title: Emergency

aid.bleedingSteps: Press firmly with a clean cloth. Do not lift it. Add more cloth on top if needed. Call 112.
aid.chokingSteps: Five back blows between the shoulder blades. Five abdominal thrusts. Repeat. Call 112.
aid.burnsSteps: Cool with running water for 10+ minutes. No butter, oil, or toothpaste. Cover loosely. Call 112 for large burns.
aid.cprSteps: Call 112 first. Push hard and fast in the centre of the chest, about 100 per minute. Continue until help arrives.
aid.seizureSteps: Clear the area. Do not hold them down. Do not put anything in their mouth. Time it. Call 112 if it lasts more than 5 minutes.
aid.poisonSteps: Call the poison centre. Do not make them vomit. Save the container or label. Call 112.

methodology.title: Clinical Governance & Safety
methodology.subtitle: How we ensure Redi Health provides safe, accurate, and culturally competent health information.
methodology.desc1: Before any AI model is called, user input is scanned against a deterministic regex engine for critical emergencies (e.g., chest pain, suicidal ideation). If triggered, the AI is bypassed entirely and the user is directed to call 112.
methodology.desc2: All messages are sanitized locally. Phone numbers, emails, IBANs, and National IDs are masked before leaving the device infrastructure.
methodology.desc3: Every lesson in the Student Health Academy undergoes clinical review by practicing Roma Health Mediators and physicians.

glossary.title: Sağlık sözlüğü
glossary.subtitle: Tıbbi terimler basit kelimelerle açıklandı.
glossary.noResults: Terim bulunamadı. Farklı bir arama deneyin.

hypertension.simple: Kan damarların duvarına çok güçlü baskı yapar. Tedavi edilmezse kalp krizi veya felce yol açabilir. İlaçlar her gün alınmalıdır.

diabetes-type-2.simple: Vücut şekeri doğru kullanamaz. Şeker kanda birikir ve organlara zarar verir. İlaç, sağlıklı yemek ve hareket gerekir. Sadece çok şeker yemekten olmaz.

anemia.simple: Yeterli sağlıklı kırmızı kan hücresi yoktur. Kendinizi yorgun, güçsüz veya baş dönmesi içinde hissedebilirsiniz. Sık nedeni demir eksikliğidir. Hamile kadınlarda çok yaygındır.

asthma.simple: Hava yolları daralır ve şişer, bu yüzden nefes almak zorlaşır. Hırıltı veya öksürük olabilir. Böyle olduğunda inhaleri kullanın. Dumandan ve tozdan uzak durun.

tuberculosis-tb.simple: Akciğerlerde ciddi bir enfeksiyondur. Haftalarca öksürürsünüz, kilo verirsiniz ve gece terlersiniz. Hava yoluyla bulaşır. 6 aylık ilaçla iyileşebilir, ama tüm hapları bitirmek gerekir.

depression.simple: Haftalar boyunca üzgün, umutsuz veya boş hissettiğiniz tıbbi bir durumdur. Bu zayıflık değildir ve sizin suçunuz değildir. İlaç ve biriyle konuşmak yardımcı olabilir. Yardım isteyin.

hepatitis-b-c.simple: Karaciğere saldıran bir virüstür. Yıllarca belirti olmayabilir ama karaciğere yavaşça zarar verir. Kan ve iğne ile bulaşır. Hepatit B için aşı vardır.

pneumonia.simple: Akciğerlerde ciddi bir enfeksiyondur. Ateş, balgamlı öksürük ve nefes darlığı olur. Doktordan antibiyotik gerekir. Bebekler ve yaşlılar için tehlikeli olabilir.

gastritis.simple: Mide iç yüzeyi tahriş olmuş veya iltihaplanmıştır. Yanma, bulantı veya şişkinlik hissedebilirsiniz. Acı yiyecek, alkol ve sigaradan uzak durun. İlaç yardımcı olabilir.

eczema.simple: Ciltte kaşıntılı, kırmızı ve kuru alanlardır. Bulaşıcı değildir. Sık sık nemlendirici kullanın. Sert sabunlardan kaçının. Doktor yardımcı olacak krem verebilir.

antibiotic.simple: Bakterileri öldüren ilaçtır. Grip veya soğuk algınlığı gibi virüslere karşı işe yaramaz. Kendinizi iyi hissetseniz bile tüm tedaviyi bitirmelisiniz, yoksa mikroplar daha güçlü döner.

ibuprofen.simple: Ağrıyı, iltihabı ve ateşi azaltan bir ilaçtır. Mideyi korumak için yemekle alın. Kutuda yazılandan fazla almayın. Hamilelikte güvenli değildir.

paracetamol.simple: Baş ağrısı ve ateş için yaygın bir ilaçtır. Çoğu insan için, hamile kadınlar dahil, güvenlidir. Günde 4 gramdan fazla almayın, çünkü karaciğere zarar verir.

insulin.simple: Vücudun şekeri kullanması için gereken hormondur. Diyabeti olan kişiler insülin iğnesine ihtiyaç duyabilir. Bu, hastalığın daha kötü olduğu anlamına gelmez; sadece tedavi farklıdır.

metformin.simple: Diyabet için en sık kullanılan ilaçtır. Vücudun şekeri daha iyi kullanmasına yardım eder. Yemekle alınır. Başta mideyi rahatsız edebilir, ama genelde geçer.

blood-test.simple: Küçük bir iğne koldan biraz kan alır. Doktor enfeksiyon, kansızlık, diyabet ve başka birçok şeyi bununla kontrol eder. Sadece kısa süre acır.

x-ray.simple: Kemiklerin ve akciğerlerin özel ışınlarla çekilen görüntüsüdür. Birkaç saniye hareketsiz durursunuz. Acıtmaz. Çoğu insan için güvenlidir, ama hamileyseniz doktora söyleyin.

ultrasound.simple: Ses dalgalarıyla vücudun içinin görüntüsüdür. Doktor cilde jel sürer ve cihazı gezdirir. Acıtmaz. Hamilelikte bebeği kontrol etmek için sık kullanılır.

ecg-ekg.simple: Kalbin ritmini kontrol eden testtir. Göğse küçük yapışkanlar yerleştirilir. Acıtmaz. Yaklaşık 5 dakika sürer. Kalbin normal atıp atmadığını gösterir.

vaccination.simple: Hastalanmadan önce vücuda hastalığa karşı savaşmayı öğreten küçük bir iğnedir. Kolda ağrı veya hafif ateş gibi küçük yan etkiler normaldir.

blood-pressure.simple: Kanın damar duvarlarına yaptığı baskıdır. Normal değer yaklaşık 120/80'dir. 140/90 üzerindeki yüksek tansiyon tehlikelidir; fark edilmeyebilir ama kalbe ve beyne zarar verir.

blood-sugar.simple: Kandaki glukoz miktarıdır. Açlıkta normal değer 70-100 mg/dL'dir. Çok yüksekse diyabet anlamına gelir. Çok düşükse baş dönmesi ve titreme yapar.

bmi.simple: Vücut kitle indeksi, kilonuzun boyunuza göre sağlıklı olup olmadığını gösterir. 18,5 altı zayıflık, 18,5-25 normal, 25 üstü fazla kilo, 30 üstü obezitedir.

immune-system.simple: Bu, vücudun mikroplara karşı ordusudur. Beyaz kan hücreleri enfeksiyonlarla savaşır. Aşılar bağışıklık sistemini eğitir. İyi beslenme, uyku ve hareket onu güçlendirir.

bcg.name: BCG (verem)
bcg.howItWorks: Bu asi bebeğin vücuduna veremle savaşmayı öğretir - havadan bulaşan ciddi bir akciğer hastalığıdır. Kola yapılan küçük bir iğne yıllarca koruma sağlar.
bcg.sideEffects: İğnenin yapıldığı yerde küçük bir kabarcık veya iz olabilir. Bu normaldir ve aşının çalıştığını gösterir.
bcg.mythDebunked: EFSANE: 'Verem artık yok.' GERÇEK: Verem hâlâ her yıl çok sayıda insanı öldürüyor. Kalabalık koşullarda hızla yayılıyor.

hepb.name: Hepatit B
hepb.howItWorks: Karaciğeri, kalıcı hasar ve kansere yol açabilen bir virüsten korur. Yenidoğanlar en savunmasız olduğu için doğumda yapılır.
hepb.sideEffects: İğne yerinde hafif ağrı olabilir. Bazı bebekler bir gün biraz huzursuz olabilir. Ciddi değildir.
hepb.mythDebunked: EFSANE: 'Bebeğim doğumda asi olmak için çok küçük.' GERÇEK: Yenidoğanlar en savunmasız gruptur. Ne kadar erken korursak o kadar iyi.

dtap.name: DTaP (difteri, tetanoz, boğmaca)
dtap.howItWorks: Bu asi aynı anda üç hastalığa karşı korur. Difteri boğazı tıkayabilir, tetanoz kasları kilitler, boğmaca ise bebeği nefessiz bırakabilir.
dtap.sideEffects: İğne yeri kızarabilir ve acıyabilir. Çocuk 1-2 gün hafif ateşli olabilir.
dtap.mythDebunked: EFSANE: 'Bu hastalıklar artık yok, neden asi olalım?' GERÇEK: Nadir olmalarının nedeni aşılamadır. Aşılama azalınca hastalıklar geri gelir.

ipv.name: Polio (IPV)
ipv.howItWorks: Polio birkaç saat içinde çocuğun bacaklarını kalıcı olarak felç edebilen bir virüstür. Bu asi hastalığı dünyanın birçok yerinden neredeyse sildi.
ipv.sideEffects: Çok hafif yan etki yapar. Bazen sadece iğne yerinde biraz kızarıklık olur.
ipv.mythDebunked: EFSANE: 'Polio bitti, bu aşıya gerek yok.' GERÇEK: Avrupa'da aşılama sayesinde bitti. Aşısız bir yolcu hastalığı geri getirebilir.

mmr.name: MMR (kızamık, kabakulak, kızamıkçık)
mmr.howItWorks: Kızamık çok bulaşıcıdır ve beyne zarar verebilir ya da öldürebilir. Bu asi tek dozla üç hastalığa da karşı korur.
mmr.sideEffects: Yaklaşık her 10 çocuktan 1'inde 7-10 gün sonra hafif ateş ve döküntü olabilir. Bu, vücudun savunmayı öğrendiğini gösterir.
mmr.mythDebunked: EFSANE: 'MMR otizme neden olur.' GERÇEK: Büyük çalışmalar otizmle hiçbir bağlantı bulmadı. Kızamık ise öldürebilir.

pneumo.name: Pnömokok aşısı (PCV13)
pneumo.howItWorks: Zatürre, menenjit ve ciddi kan enfeksiyonlarına yol açan bakterilere karşı korur. Bunlar küçük çocuklar için en tehlikeli hastalıklardandır.
pneumo.sideEffects: 1-2 gün hafif ateş, huzursuzluk ve iğne yerinde ağrı olabilir.
pneumo.mythDebunked: EFSANE: 'Çocuklar çok aşıdan hastalanır.' GERÇEK: Çocuğun bağışıklık sistemi her gün binlerce mikropla karşılaşır. Birkaç asi onun için fazla değildir.

rota.name: Rotavirüs
rota.howItWorks: İğne değil, ağızdan damla olarak verilir. Ağır ishale ve susuz kalmaya yol açan virüse karşı korur.
rota.sideEffects: Çok hafif yan etkiler olur. Bazen biraz huzursuzluk veya hafif ishal görülebilir.
rota.mythDebunked: EFSANE: 'İshal bebeklerde normaldir, aşı gerekmez.' GERÇEK: Rotavirüs ishali çocuğu hızlıca susuz bırakabilir ve tehlikeli olabilir.

varicella.name: Suçiçeği
varicella.howItWorks: Suçiçeği hafif görünebilir ama ciddi cilt enfeksiyonu, zatürre veya beyinle ilgili komplikasyonlara yol açabilir. Bu asi vakaların çoğunu önler.
varicella.sideEffects: Hafif ağrı olabilir. Nadiren iğne yerinin yakınında birkaç suçiçeği benzeri nokta çıkar.
varicella.mythDebunked: EFSANE: 'Suçiçeğini doğal geçirmek daha iyidir.' GERÇEK: Doğal enfeksiyon ciddi komplikasyonlara yol açabilir ve ileride zona yapabilir.

hpv.name: HPV (insan papilloma virüsü)
hpv.howItWorks: Bu asi kanseri önler. Gençler virüsle karşılaşmadan önce yapılır.
hpv.sideEffects: Kolda ağrı, bazen kısa baş ağrısı veya baş dönmesi olabilir. Çok güvenlidir.
hpv.mythDebunked: EFSANE: 'Bu asi cinsel davranışı teşvik eder.' GERÇEK: Bu asi KANSERİ önler. Davranışla ilgisi yoktur.

flu.name: Grip
flu.howItWorks: Grip sadece soğuk algınlığı değildir. Küçük çocuklar, yaşlılar ve hamile kadınlar için tehlikeli olabilir. Bu asi her yıl güncellenir.
flu.sideEffects: Kolda hafif ağrı ve bazen bir gün süren düşük ateş olabilir. Bu aşıdan grip OLMAZSINIZ.
flu.mythDebunked: EFSANE: 'Grip aşısı grip yapar.' GERÇEK: Bu imkânsızdır. Aşıda canlı virüs yoktur.
